question.text: Under the Negotiable Instruments Act 1881, a cheque is defined under Section 6 as:

answer.text: An instrument in writing containing an unconditional order to pay a certain sum of money to the bearer
An instrument in writing containing an unconditional promise to pay a certain sum of money
A bill of exchange drawn on a specified banker and payable only on demand
A negotiable instrument that can be drawn on any person and is payable on a specified future date

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Section 6 of the Negotiable Instruments Act 1881 defines a cheque as "a bill of exchange drawn on a specified banker and payable only on demand." This distinguishes a cheque from other negotiable instruments — it must be drawn on a banker (not any person) and must be payable on demand (not at a future date). **The Trap:** The second option describes a promissory note (Section 4), not a cheque. The first option is a partial description of a bill of exchange. Candidates often confuse the definitions of promissory notes, bills of exchange, and cheques. **Cross-Exam Context:** Definitions under the Negotiable Instruments Act are tested in NISM Series X-A and banking-related certifications. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When accepting cheques from clients for advisory fees, remember that a cheque is payable on demand — post-dated cheques are technically not cheques until the date written on them arrives.
